rows.v: The first Chief Justice of the United States (1789–1795), a Founding Father who helped shape the new federal judiciary.
Jay led a Court with little defined power, and resigned to become governor of New York, then seen as a more important post.
A principal author of the Federalist Papers, he later negotiated the Jay Treaty with Britain, a controversial cornerstone of early foreign policy.
The fourth and longest-serving Chief Justice (1801–1835), regarded as the most important in the Court's history.
Marshall's ruling in Marbury v. Madison established judicial review, the Court's power to strike down unconstitutional laws.
Over 34 years he transformed the Supreme Court from a weak body into a coequal branch of government, defining its authority for all time.
The fifth Chief Justice (1836–1864), whose tenure is forever defined by one catastrophic decision.
Taney authored the Dred Scott decision, ruling that Black Americans could not be citizens, a ruling that helped precipitate the Civil War.
Though he had a long career and earlier reputation for ability, that single 1857 opinion stands as one of the most reviled in American legal history.
The sixth Chief Justice (1864–1873), who presided over the nation during Reconstruction.
Chase presided over the impeachment trial of President Andrew Johnson, lending the proceedings judicial gravity.
A former Treasury secretary and abolitionist, he had ambitions for the presidency himself even while leading the Court.
The eighth Chief Justice (1888–1910), who led the Court through the Gilded Age.
Fuller's Court issued rulings that limited federal economic regulation, reflecting the era's laissez-faire philosophy.
His long tenure spanned a period of rapid industrialization, with a Court often siding with business against reform efforts.
The tenth Chief Justice (1921–1930) and the only person to lead both the executive and judicial branches.
Taft is the only person to serve as both President of the United States and Chief Justice, a unique distinction.
He considered the Chief Justiceship the greater honor, and successfully lobbied for the Court's own building, separating it from Congress.
The 11th Chief Justice (1930–1941), who steered the Court through the constitutional crisis of the New Deal.
Hughes navigated President Roosevelt's court-packing threat, presiding as the Court shifted to uphold New Deal legislation.
He had earlier resigned from the Court to run for president, narrowly losing in 1916, before returning years later to lead it.
The 14th Chief Justice (1953–1969), who led one of the most transformative and activist eras in the Court's history.
Warren's Court issued Brown v. Board of Education, ending legal school segregation, and expanded civil liberties dramatically.
A former California governor, his tenure reshaped American law on civil rights, criminal procedure, and the principle of one person, one vote.
The 15th Chief Justice (1969–1986), who led a Court that was more moderate than expected.
Burger wrote the unanimous opinion in United States v. Nixon, forcing the president to release the Watergate tapes.
Though appointed to rein in the liberal Warren Court, his tenure preserved many of its precedents while addressing new constitutional questions.
The 16th Chief Justice (1986–2005), who guided the Court in a more conservative direction over nearly two decades.
Rehnquist championed federalism and states' rights, and presided over the impeachment trial of President Bill Clinton.
Elevated from associate justice, his long leadership marked a deliberate shift away from the expansive rulings of the Warren era.
The 17th and current Chief Justice (2005–present), known for his concern with the Court's institutional legitimacy.
Roberts cast a decisive vote to uphold the Affordable Care Act, surprising many and reflecting his institutionalist instincts.
He famously likened a judge's role to a baseball umpire calling balls and strikes, a metaphor for judicial restraint that has defined his public image.
An associate justice (1902–1932) often called one of the greatest legal minds in American history.
Holmes's incisive opinions and dissents, prizing judicial restraint and free speech, profoundly shaped American law.
Known as 'The Great Dissenter,' his eloquent minority opinions frequently became the law of the land generations later.
An associate justice (1916–1939), the first Jewish member of the Court and a champion of individual rights.
Brandeis pioneered the use of real-world social and economic data in legal arguments, the famous 'Brandeis brief.'
A fierce critic of monopoly and defender of privacy, his appointment was bitterly contested but proved enormously influential.
An associate justice (1937–1971) known for his absolutist defense of the Bill of Rights.
Black argued the First Amendment's protections were absolute, reading 'no law' abridging free speech to mean exactly that.
A former senator, he was a leading force in applying the Bill of Rights to the states, expanding individual liberties nationwide.
An associate justice (1939–1962) and leading advocate of judicial restraint.
Frankfurter believed courts should defer to elected legislatures, often clashing with colleagues who favored bolder intervention.
An immigrant and former Harvard Law professor, he was a towering intellectual whose philosophy of restraint influenced generations of jurists.
The longest-serving justice in the Court's history (1939–1975), a fierce defender of civil liberties and the environment.
Douglas served over 36 years and authored the opinion finding a constitutional right to privacy in Griswold v. Connecticut.
A passionate individualist and outdoorsman, his record-setting tenure and bold libertarian streak made him one of the Court's most colorful figures.
An associate justice (1956–1990) and one of the most influential liberal voices of the modern Court.
Brennan was a master coalition-builder whose opinions shaped law on free speech, civil rights, and criminal justice.
Appointed by a Republican president, he became a leading architect of the Warren Court's revolution, famously counting votes on his fingers — 'five.'
The first Black Supreme Court Justice (1967–1991), and the lawyer who had argued and won Brown v. Board of Education.
Before joining the Court, Marshall won the landmark case ending school segregation as the nation's leading civil rights attorney.
His appointment broke a centuries-old color barrier, and on the bench he remained a passionate champion of equality and individual rights.
The first woman to serve on the Supreme Court (1981–2006), often the decisive swing vote in closely divided cases.
O'Connor's pragmatic, case-by-case approach made her the pivotal vote on issues from abortion to affirmative action for years.
Her appointment shattered the Court's all-male history, and her centrist influence made her, for a time, arguably the most powerful jurist in America.
An associate justice (1993–2020), a pioneering advocate for gender equality who became a cultural icon.
Before the Court, Ginsburg argued landmark gender-discrimination cases; on it, she became a powerful, pointed dissenter.
Affectionately nicknamed 'The Notorious R.B.G.,' her fierce dissents and lifelong fight for equal rights made her a beloved public figure.
The first Latina and first Hispanic Supreme Court Justice (2009–present).
Sotomayor's appointment marked a historic milestone, and she has become a leading liberal voice known for her pointed dissents.
Raised in a Bronx housing project, her path from humble beginnings to the nation's highest court embodies a powerful American story.
The first Black woman to serve on the Supreme Court (2022–present).
Jackson's confirmation broke a historic barrier, adding the perspective of a former public defender to the bench.
A graduate of Harvard and a former federal judge, her appointment continued the slow diversification of an institution long dominated by white men.
An associate justice (1991–present) and a leading conservative voice, known for his originalist philosophy.
Thomas advocates interpreting the Constitution by its original public meaning, and is often the Court's most willing to revisit precedent.
Only the second Black justice, his contentious confirmation and his long, influential conservative tenure have made him a defining figure.
An associate justice (1986–2016) and the intellectual champion of originalism and textualism.
Scalia argued laws and the Constitution should be read by their text and original meaning, transforming American legal interpretation.
His vivid, combative writing and forceful dissents made him perhaps the most influential conservative jurist of his generation.
An associate justice (1988–2018) who served for decades as the Court's pivotal swing vote.
Kennedy authored the landmark opinion in Obergefell v. Hodges, establishing a nationwide right to same-sex marriage.
His unpredictable, deciding vote in major cases on liberty and equality gave him outsized influence over the law for a generation.
An associate justice (1994–2022) known for a pragmatic approach focused on a law's real-world consequences.
Breyer argued for interpreting laws by their purpose and practical effects, a counterpoint to the originalism of his colleagues.
A consensus-seeker who valued the Court's collegiality, he stepped down after nearly three decades to allow a timely successor.
An associate justice (2006–present) and a reliably conservative voice on the Court.
Alito authored the 2022 opinion in Dobbs that overturned Roe v. Wade, ending the constitutional right to abortion.
A former prosecutor and appeals judge, his consequential majority opinions have made him a central figure in the Court's conservative turn.
An associate justice (2010–present), a former Solicitor General and Harvard Law dean known for her sharp, accessible writing.
Kagan is regarded as one of the Court's most effective writers, crafting clear, pointed opinions and dissents.
She was the first justice in decades appointed without prior experience as a judge, having instead led one of the nation's top law schools.
An associate justice (2017–present), an originalist in the mold of the seat's previous occupant.
Gorsuch surprised observers by authoring a ruling extending federal civil rights protections to LGBTQ workers, on textualist grounds.
Known for his strong defense of textualism, his independent streak occasionally produces rulings that cross the Court's usual ideological lines.
An associate justice (2020–present), a former law professor and the Court's youngest current member at her appointment.
Barrett, a self-described originalist, was a student and clerk of Antonin Scalia, whose interpretive philosophy she shares.
Her confirmation just before the 2020 election solidified a conservative majority, marking a significant moment in the Court's modern trajectory.
